name: South Korea

colors.symbolism: peace, purity and the traditional clothing of the Korean people
yang — the positive cosmic force; warmth, life, and the sun
eum (yin) — the negative cosmic force; cold, water, and balance
constancy, justice, vigilance — the colour of the four trigrams

description: A white field bearing a central red-and-blue Taegeuk (yin-yang circle, with red over blue divided by an S-curve), surrounded at the four corners by black trigrams: heaven (Geon, three solid bars) at the upper hoist, water (Gam) at the upper fly, fire (Ri) at the lower hoist, and earth (Gon, six broken bars) at the lower fly.
history: Known as the Taegeukgi, the flag was first designed in 1882 by Bak Yeong-hyo, a diplomat carrying credentials to Imperial Japan, and was officially adopted by the Joseon Kingdom on 6 March 1883. After the Japanese annexation (1910–1945) it was the rallying flag of the Korean independence movement. The Republic of Korea formally readopted it on 12 July 1948 with detailed specifications; the current standardised version (with mathematically defined Taegeuk and trigram proportions) was promulgated by Presidential Decree on 25 October 1949 and revised in 1984, 1997 and 2007.

features.symbolism: peace and purity — long associated with the Korean people, who have called themselves the 'people of white clothing' (baegui-minjok)
the central red-and-blue circle is the Taegeuk, depicting the cosmic balance of yang (red, upper) and eum/yin (blue, lower) — the universe in dynamic equilibrium

four of the eight trigrams of the I Ching surround the Taegeuk: ☰ Geon (heaven; three solid bars) at the upper hoist, ☷ Gon (earth; six broken bars) at the lower fly, ☵ Gam (water/moon; two broken bars flanking a solid one) at the upper fly, and ☲ Ri (fire/sun; two solid bars flanking a broken one) at the lower hoist; together they signify the four cardinal elements, seasons, virtues and directions
